content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 14, 2009

Daniel Miller
Filing Officer
Marin County Registrar of Voters
P.O. Box E
San Rafael, CA 94913

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-09-253

Dear Mr. Miller:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the campaign reporting provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your questions seek general guidance, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

QUESTIONS

1. May local jurisdictions pass regulations or ordinances that specify filing requirements and establish local enforcement procedures for the failure to file, which supersede enforcement proceedings set forth in the Act.

2. May local jurisdictions establish a single central filing agency to review and process campaign statements for both city and county jurisdictions.

CONCLUSIONS

1. While the Act does not entirely prohibit local regulations or ordinances imposing local requirements or enforcement procedures, the adopted regulations or ordinances must be consistent with the Act, may not interfere with filings required by the Act, and may only apply to candidates and committees within the jurisdiction. In addition, duly adopted local regulations or

___________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Please note that informal assistance does not provide the requestor with immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-09-253
Page No. 2

ordinances imposing additional filing requirements or permitting local enforcement proceedings
do not supersede, and will have no affect on, filings required under the Act or enforcement
proceedings initiated under the Act by the Commission or other prosecuting authorities.

2. While the Act does not prohibit local jurisdictions from establishing a central filing
agency to review and process campaign statements, a local jurisdiction may transfer a filing
officer's duties to the central filing agency only in respects to candidates and committees within
the jurisdiction. Because a county may not transfer the county elections official's duties as the filing
officer for candidate controlled committees for statewide offices or offices with jurisdiction in
parts of two or more counties, primarily formed committees supporting or opposing candidates
or measures to be voted upon in parts of two or more counties, and state general purpose
committees, the only fully viable option for establishing a single central filing agency is for cities
within the County of Marin to transfer their city clerks' duties as filing officers to the office of
the county's elections official.

FACTS

You are the filing officer for the County of Marin. In your role as the filing officer, you
have noticed a small number of candidates/officials who refuse to file campaign statements as
required by the Act. To address this concern with candidates/officials who fail to file, you would
like assistance as to whether the Act allows local jurisdictions to pass regulations or ordinances
that specify filing guidelines and establish local enforcement proceedings that supersede
enforcement proceedings set forth in the Act.

In addition, you ask whether the Act permits local jurisdictions to establish a single
central filing agency to review and process campaign finance statements for both city and county
jurisdictions. Under this proposal, city clerks would participate voluntarily and have the right to
opt out at any time.

ANALYSIS

Section 81013 of the Act provides the following:

"Nothing in this title prevents the Legislature or any other state or local
agency from imposing additional requirements on any person if the requirements
do not prevent the person from complying with this title. If any act of the
Legislature conflicts with the provisions of this title, this title shall prevail."

Additionally, Section 81009.5(b) provides:

"Notwithstanding Section 81013, no local government agency shall enact
any ordinance imposing filing requirements additional to or different from those
set forth in Chapter 4 (commencing with Section 84100) for elections held in its
jurisdiction unless the additional or different filing requirements apply only to the
candidates seeking election in that jurisdiction, their controlled committees or

File No. I-09-253
Page No. 3

committees formed or existing primarily to support or oppose their candidacies,
and to committees formed or existing primarily to support or oppose a candidate
or to support or oppose the qualification of, or passage of, a local ballot measure
which is being voted on only in that jurisdiction, and to city or county general
purpose committees active only in that city or county, respectively."

As a general rule, a local jurisdiction is permitted to impose additional requirements so
long as the requirements do not prevent a person from complying with the Act and apply only to
candidates and committees within the jurisdiction. (Sections 81013 and 81090.5.)² However,
when provisions of local law conflict with the state law, the appropriate provisions of state law
supersede them. (See Cederdahl Advice Letter, No. I-92-467, citing Cohen v. Board of
Supervisors (1985) 40 Cal.3d 277 and In re Hubbard (1964) 62 Cal.2d 119.) In many cases,
therefore, a local governmental agency may not enact an ordinance which differs from and
supersedes the Act. (In re Miller (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 9l.) To make this determination, the
provisions of the local law must be examined on a provision-by-provision basis.

Additionally, under some circumstances, a local requirement might be invalid under
Section 81013 even if an ordinance is consistent with the Act. For example, we have advised
that when local filing requirements become so "burdensome or confusing" that they seriously
interfere with filings required by the Act, the state law preempts the local ordinance. (Nielsen
Advice Letter, No. A-82-006.)

1. Local regulations or ordinances that specify filing requirements and establish local
enforcement proceedings.

While the Act does not entirely prohibit local regulations or ordinances imposing local
filing requirements or enforcement proceedings, the regulations or ordinances must be consistent
with the Act, may not interfere with filings required by the Act, and may apply only to
candidates and committees within the jurisdiction. Candidates and committees within the
jurisdiction are limited to the following:

• Candidate controlled committees for elected office within the jurisdiction.
• Committees primarily formed to support or oppose a single candidate or measure, or
two or more candidates or measures in the same election, to be voted on solely within
the jurisdiction as defined by Regulation 18247.5(a).
• General purpose committee within the jurisdiction as defined by Regulation
18247.5(b).

² Notably, charter cities are granted "home rule" powers under Article XI, Section 5 of the state
Constitution. Under this authority, a charter city's regulation or ordinance implicating a "municipal affair" is
superseded by state law only if there is an actual conflict and only if the state statute is one of "statewide concern"
that is "reasonably related" and "narrowly tailored" to its resolution. (See Johnson v. Bradley (1992) 4 Cal. 4th 389.)
At this time, we express no opinion as to whether charter cities may impose additional filing requirements,
other than those specified by the Act, upon persons or committees active within the jurisdiction but not a committee
subject to filing with the city under Section 84215(e).

File No. 1-09-253
Page No. 4

However, because you have not limited your request for assistance to a specific
regulation or ordinance, we are unable to determine whether any proposed action by the County
of Marin is consistent or interferes with the Act. If you need additional assistance to determine
whether any particular regulation or ordinance is consistent or interferes with the Act, you should
seek additional advice providing all relevant facts. Moreover, we emphasize that duly adopted
local regulations or ordinances imposing additional filing requirements or permitting local
enforcement proceedings do not supersede, and will have no affect on, filings required under the
Act or enforcement proceedings initiated under the Act by the Commission or other prosecuting
authorities. (See Sections 91001 and 91001.5.)

2. Single central filing agency to review and process campaign statements for both city
and county jurisdictions.

The Act requires the filing of campaign statements and reports by many persons. (See
Sections 84200 et seq.) Section 84215 addresses where these campaign statements and reports
must be filed and provides the following in pertinent part:

"All candidates and elected officers and their controlled committees, except as
provided in subdivision (e), shall file one copy of the campaign statements
required by Section 84200 with the elections official of the county in which the
candidate or elected officer is domiciled, as defined in subdivision (b) of Section
349 of the Elections Code. In addition, campaign statements shall be filed at the
following places:

"(a) Statewide elected officers and candidates for these offices other than the
Board of Equalization, supreme court justices, their controlled committees,
committees formed or existing primarily to support or oppose these candidates,
elected officers, supreme court justices, or statewide measures, or the
qualification of state ballot measures, and all state general purpose committees
and filers not specified in subdivisions (b) to (e), inclusive:
"(1) The original and one copy with the Secretary of State.
"(2) One copy with the Registrar-Recorder of Los Angeles County.
"(3) One copy with the Registrar of Voters of the City and County of San
Francisco.
"(b) Members of the Legislature or Board of Equalization, court of appeal
justices, superior court judges, candidates for those offices, their controlled
committees, and committees formed or existing primarily to support or oppose
these candidates or officeholders:
"(1) The original and one copy with the Secretary of State.
"(2) One copy with the elections official of the county with the largest
number of registered voters in the districts affected.
"(c) Elected officers in jurisdictions other than legislative districts, Board of
Equalization districts, or appellate court districts that contain parts of two or more
counties, candidates for these offices, their controlled committees, and
committees formed or existing primarily to support or oppose candidates or local

File No. I-09-253
Page No. 5

measures to be voted upon in one of these jurisdictions shall file the original and
one copy with the elections official of the county with the largest number of
registered voters in the jurisdiction.
"(d) County elected officers, candidates for these offices, their controlled
committees, committees formed or existing primarily to support or oppose
candidates or local measures to be voted upon in any number of jurisdictions
within one county, other than those specified in subdivision (e), and county
general purpose committees shall file the original and one copy with the elections
official of the county.
"(e) City elected officers, candidates for city office, their controlled committees,
committees formed or existing primarily to support or oppose candidates or local
measures to be voted upon in one city, and city general purpose committees shall
file the original and one copy with the clerk of the city. These elected officers,
candidates, and committees need not file with the elections official of the county
in which they are domiciled."

As discussed above, local filing requirements are permitted only to the extent that they
are consistent with the Act, do not interfere with filings required under the Act, and apply only to
candidates and committees within the jurisdiction. Previously, we have determined that the Act
prohibited a charter amendment by the City and County of San Francisco transferring the filing
officer's duties from the San Francisco Registrar of Voters to the San Francisco Ethics
Commission. (Moll Advice Letter, No. A-96-315.) However, we later determined that this
transfer was prohibited only to the extent that the City and County of San Francisco were
attempting to change the filing locations prescribed by the Act for candidates and committees not
within their jurisdiction. For filing obligations that applied only to candidates and committees within
a local jurisdiction, we have previously determined that the City and County of San Francisco's
designation of a different location for campaign reports and statements, other than the location
required under Section 84215, was "a valid exercise of a local jurisdiction's authority under
Section 81009.5." (Sutton Advice Letter, No. A-01-184.)

While the Act does not prohibit local jurisdictions from establishing a single central filing
agency to review and process campaign statements, a local jurisdiction may transfer a filing
officer's duties to the central filing agency only in regards to candidates and committees within
the jurisdiction. Because city clerks' duties as filing officers are limited to candidates and
committees within their cities under Section 84215(e), cities may transfer their clerks' duties as
filing officers to other offices, the counties in which the cities are located, or joint agencies of the
cities and counties in which the cities are located without restriction. Accordingly, cities
within the County of Marin may transfer their filing officer duties from their city clerks to a
central filing agency within the county or the office of the county's elections official.

A county, however, may only transfer its elections official's duties as a filing officer for
candidate controlled committees for county offices, committees primarily formed to support or
oppose candidates or measures to be voted upon solely within the county, and county general
purpose committees. A county may not transfer its elections official's duties as the filing officer
for candidate controlled committees for statewide offices or offices with jurisdiction in parts of

File No. 1-09-253
Page No. 6

two or more counties, primarily formed committees supporting or opposing candidates or
measures to be voted upon in parts of two or more counties, or state general purpose committees.
Accordingly, the County of Marin may not transfer all of its elections official's duties as a filing
officer to a central filing agency within the county other than the office of the county's elections
official. If you seek to have a single central filing agency for both city and county jurisdictions,
the only fully viable option is for cities within the county to transfer their city clerks' duties as
filing officers to the office of the county's elections official.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division
BGL:jgl
